data.title: REGL: Low Valuation, Track Record Not So Strong
data.description:
The ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (BATS:REGL) is a niche mid-cap fund targeting dividend-paying companies with a tilt toward financials and utilities. Sure, its defensiveness and low valuation draw attention in the current environment. On the other hand, despite a slowing economy, positive corporate earnings have been a strong support for the stock market. This mixed scenario makes REGL an option for a value-oriented portfolio, but not the best opportunity for investors seeking competitive returns in the mid-cap category.

ETF Description & Highlights

REGL tracks the S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats Index, which is designed by S&P Dow Jones Indices to offer exposure to U.S. mid-sized companies that have raised dividends annually for at least 15 years. The index is a subset of the S&P MidCap 400 and is composed of at least 40 equally weighted companies. To ensure the index reflects current market conditions, constituents are subject to quarterly rebalances.

As of August 1, 2025, REGL managed $1.8 billion in assets across 53 holdings, with an average market cap of $7.57 billion. While the S&P MidCap 400 index’s definition of mid caps follows S&P Dow Jones standards, the table below shows the market cap breakdown according to Morningstar methodology, which is based on the percentage of the total market cap, where mega caps are the top 40% of total market capitalization, large caps cover the next 30%, mid caps the following 20%, small caps the next 7%, and micro caps the last 3%.

Therefore, according to Morningstar, REGL has 11.5% of the fund in mid caps and 86.5% in small caps. For comparison, the S&P MidCap 400, here represented by the iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH), has mid caps accounting for 26.9% and small caps 69.7%, which gives REGL a bias toward smaller capitalization companies compared to the benchmark.

Given its equal-weighted structure, deviations in weights from its constituents are expected to be minimal and more of a result of short-term price moves after the last rebalance. At the moment, though, the largest positions are mostly utilities and financial companies, which account for six out of the ten holdings, such as Portland General Electric, NorthWestern Energy, Black Hills, Essential Utilities, UGI, and Equity Lifestyle Properties. As a result, despite REGL’s relatively concentrated portfolio, its top ten holdings represent only 20% of the fund.

The table below includes a list of six ETFs primarily focused on mid caps that will serve as the peer group for REGL in this analysis. The first is the Vanguard Mid-Cap ETF (VO), the largest fund in the group, which employs a market-cap weighted approach to track the CRSP US Mid Cap Index. Next, the iShares Russell Mid-Cap ETF (IWR) offers a broad-based coverage of the segment with over 800 holdings, following the Russell MidCap Index. The following funds have a bias toward value and quality, which are usually characteristics seen in dividend-paying companies. The Invesco S&P MidCap Quality ETF (XMHQ) favors companies with strong fundamentals, including return on equity, accruals, and leverage. On the other hand, the iShares Russell Mid-Cap Value ETF (IWS) and iShares S&P Mid-Cap 400 Value ETF (IJJ) both select stocks based on valuation, following different index methodologies. IWS is a more diversified fund with nearly 700 constituents, while IJJ has a greater allocation to smaller capitalization companies. Completing the list, the WisdomTree US MidCap Dividend ETF (DON) selects companies based on dividend yields, which makes it a fund with the closest investment strategy relative to REGL.

REGL’s largest exposure is to financial services, which makes up 30.5% of the portfolio. That is followed by industrials at 17.3%, utilities at 17.9%, and consumer discretionary at 9.3%. The fund also has modest weights in healthcare (7.5%), basic materials (5.6%), energy (4.0%), real estate (3.9%), and consumer staples (3.9%). Compared to the S&P MidCap 400 index, REGL is significantly overweight in utilities (+15.3%) and financial services (+13.7%), which is consistent with its dividend-oriented profile. The flip side is its underweight allocation to industrials (-5.7%), consumer discretionary (-4.0%), and its lack of exposure to technology, which contrasts with the benchmark 14.4% position in the sector.

Breaking down the fund’s allocation by sector, we see that in financial services, there is a focused allocation around regional banks and insurance names, two areas that typically pay high dividends. Core positions include mid-sized lenders like UMB Financial, International Bancshares, and Prosperity Bancshares. In the insurance industry, the fund holds positions in Old Republic International and American Financial Group, along with property and casualty insurers such as Hanover Insurance and RLI Corp. The exposure to financials is complemented by a position in SEI Investments, adding exposure to the asset management segment.

The allocation to the industrials sector has a clear tilt toward machinery, accounting for almost half of the sector through holdings in Lincoln Electric, Donaldson, and MSA Safety, among others. The fund still has significant positions in trading and distribution companies, holding shares of RB Global and Applied Industrial Technologies, while a stake in Toro gives exposure to the agricultural machinery segment.

In utilities, the fund spreads its allocation across ten holdings, including gas utilities and multi-utilities. Core names include UGI Corp, New Jersey Resources, and Spire. Unlike the S&P MidCap 400, which has several electric utilities in the index, REGL’s only position in this industry is Portland General Electric, a company that pays dividends yielding nearly 5%.

Beyond these largest sectors, REGL still has significant exposure to consumer discretionary, with holdings in Polaris, Thor Industries, and RH, while positions in the healthcare provider Ensign Group, along with Royal Gold, ROM International, and Westlake, add diversification across areas like healthcare and materials.

Compared to the peer group of mid-cap ETFs, we can say that REGL remains overweight in financial services and utilities. In general, market cap-weighted funds VO and IWR have a sector distribution well in line with the S&P MidCap 400. Meanwhile, the quality-oriented XMHQ has a clear bias toward industrials and healthcare. This contrasts with IWS and IJJ, two value ETFs that maintain an allocation style not far away from VO and IWR, as these funds are only slightly underweighted in high-multiple consumer discretionary stocks and especially technology. Curiously, the technology sector’s participation in mid-cap indexes is not as high as in broader market indexes, such as the S&P 500, where technology represents over 30% of the index. As a result, REGL’s sector correlation with IWS and IJJ is only modest at nearly 65%.

Meanwhile, as expected, the dividend-focused DON has a much higher allocation alignment with REGL, driven by its large stance in financial services (25.0%) and a limited exposure to technology (5.0%), driving a sector correlation of 86% with REGL.

High Dividends

REGL’s dividend yield of 2.3% is in line with DON (2.4%), but much higher than market cap funds (VO and IWR), and even IWS and IJJ, as their value tilt has only slightly increased these funds’ yields.

Meanwhile, it is a bit surprising to me that REGL, a fund that targets dividend growth, has seen modest dividend growth compared to DON and value funds IWS and IJJ. On the other hand, VO and IWR’s higher dividend growth is somewhat expected, driven by their greater allocation to higher growth companies, which usually reinvest available resources to fund growth rather than return it to shareholders as dividends.

Attractive Valuation

REGL’s 15.3x P/E represents an 11.4% discount to the S&P MidCap 400’s 17.3x. This valuation gap reflects the fund’s overweight exposure to financial services, a sector that is trading below 13x P/E. Its lack of exposure to the technology sector also drags valuations down, given the sector’s valuation typically north of 25x P/E. It is also worth noting the low valuation profile of holdings in utilities, such as Portland General Electric and National Fuel Gas, both below 13x P/E.

Growth is certainly not REGL’s strength. Historical earnings growth (0.7%), earnings forecast (9.5%), and sales growth (2.7%) all lag the S&P MidCap 400, driven by weaker growth across several sectors, including consumer staples, healthcare, and energy, not to mention its lack of exposure to technology, an usually high-growth sector.

Meanwhile, profitability is mixed. REGL’s EBITDA margin of 27.6% is 4.2 percentage points higher than the S&P MidCap 400, but its ROE (14.8%) trails the index’s 16.6%. On the positive side, high EBITDA margins in utilities (nearly 38%) and stronger margins in real estate are core contributors. At the same time, weak EBITDA margins and ROE in consumer discretionary do not help, offsetting the healthy numbers in other sectors.

Looking at the peer group valuations, we can see that VO (P/E 19.9) and IWR (P/E 18.9x) multiples are even higher than the S&P MidCap 400. This suggests that the S&P MidCap 400 (P/E 17.3x) appears as a cheaper slice of the mid-cap space, likely driven by its greater emphasis on smaller capitalization companies. In addition, XHMQ’s P/E of 19.3 reflects that quality is not influencing valuations as much, while funds like IWS and IJJ have P/E ratios below 17x as a result of their value approach. In this context, REGL and DON both sit in the value territory as well, influenced by their high exposure to financial services.

Underperforming The Category

REGL has lagged behind the S&P MidCap 400 over the past years, with annualized returns of 11.4% (5-year) and 7.8% (3-year) versus 12.3% and 9.8% for the S&P MidCap 400. REGL has also been a laggard among peers, including DON and value funds IWS and IJJ.

The fund’s low beta profile has been a key factor behind its underperformance. REGL beta of 0.84 (5-year) and 0.78 (3-year) is much lower than the benchmark and all peers, IWS and IJJ, which have seen betas above 1.00, indicating a relatively high volatility profile for value funds.

REGL’s capture ratios reveal, however, that the fund has generally followed the broader market during declines, with downside capture ratios in the range of 98 to 102, outperforming peers. On the other hand, at least over the past three years, it has missed much of the upside (capture ratio of 70), trailing value funds DON (85), value funds IWS (89), and IJJ (93) as well.

REGL’s average daily volume of $3.53 million is modest, but is well lower than all peers, including DON. This results in a 0.12% average bid-ask spread, the highest in the group. While its turnover of 33% is in the middle of the pack, the fund has the highest expense ratio (0.40%), a high cost for an ETF that tracks an equally weighted index. The only reason may come from its quarterly rebalances. Lastly, a tracking error at around 10% is at the upper end of the peers’ range, while its Sharpe ratio of 0.21 (3-year) and 0.58 (5-year) are the lowest in the group, indicating that its lower volatility has been overshadowed by weaker returns, particularly during the overall bullish environment that we have experienced in the past years.

Final Thoughts

REGL plays a clear role in the mid-cap space. It offers exposure to companies with a proven dividend track record. A bias toward financial services and utilities gives the fund a conservative tilt that bodes well for investors looking to mitigate volatility in a somewhat noisy market. While its defensiveness sounds appealing at this moment, given mixed economic signals and elevated valuations, it comes at the cost of limited upside during rallies, which has hurt the fund’s performance over the past years.

In this environment, REGL‘s cautious profile may still resonate with income-oriented investors, considering expectations for rate cuts in the coming months. However, this fund is not the best option for those expecting outperformance in the mid-cap category.

data.title: CCD: Currently Paying Out More Than It Earns
data.description:
Overview

Income funds can be valuable holdings during periods of market uncertainty and volatility due to their better price stability. Now that President Trump's tariffs are in effect, investors are unsure of how different industries will react. I believe that a fund like Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income Fund (NASDAQ:CCD) can be an efficient way to hedge your portfolio against the uncertainty going forward. CCD aims to provide attractive total returns through its portfolio of convertible and fixed income securities. I previously covered CCD and issued a hold rating due to its premium valuation and interest rate sensitivity.

Since my prior coverage, CCD recently released an updated semi-annual report, which prompted me to revisit this fund to provide some updated insights into its performance. Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CCD's share price has declined by about 8.6%. Even when including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return is a bit lackluster at 0.6% over the same period. CCD offers investors a generous starting dividend yield of 11.4%, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking a high level of income generation within their portfolio.

However, the fund's most recent reporting seems to indicate that CCD is having trouble generating a sufficient level of income to support distributions. As a result of its weaker performance in recent months, the fund's premium to NAV valuation has significantly reduced. Despite this, I do not believe that CCD has reached buy territory yet. CCD has an inception dating back to 2015, which means we have over a decade of operating history that we can reference.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, CCD has total managed assets of $807M that are spread across a diverse range of sectors. CCD also has a management fee of 1%, excluding any other fees related to interest. The fund chooses to focus on a portfolio of convertible securities and high-yield fixed income assets as a way to generate attractive total returns and income generation for shareholders.

A 'convertible' security simply means that CCD maintains exposure to preferred shares of bonds that were issued by a company. As the name implies, these convertible securities can be converted into a specific amount of common shares, if CCD believes that the underlying holding has potential for capital appreciation. So while income generation is priority, CCD keeps this structure in a scenario where the investment shows strong potential for capital appreciation, which allows CCD to get the best of both worlds.

As we can see below, CCD is most heavily allocated towards investments in the Information Technology sector, which accounts for 34.4%. This makes sense because technology companies tend to demonstrate the highest level of capital appreciation, so CCD can convert these securities to common shares if growth occurs. This exposure is followed by weight in Consumer Discretionary and Health Care investments, which account for 12.2% and 10.1% of assets respectively.

Looking at the holdings below, we can see many recognizable large-cap companies. For instance, the largest position is within The Boeing Company (BA) with a weight of 2.8% of assets. This is followed by other notable names like Snowflake (SNOW), Strategy (MSTR), and Uber Technologies (UBER) just to name a few. However, we must remember that most of these positions are held as convertible bonds, so investors should still limit their expectations around the fund's ability to provide capital appreciation. As we can see below, CCD still holds these positions as convertible bonds and not as common shares, so growth will be limited.

Updated Valuation & Risk Profile

At the time of my last coverage, CCD traded at a large premium to NAV of 16.8%. Following the continuous price decline, CCD's premium to NAV has fallen to approximately 4.5%. CCD has traded at an average premium to NAV of 13.19% over the last three-year period, but I still do not believe that entry here is enticing yet. To help visualize its current valuation, I added a red line in the chart below to demonstrate that CCD still remains at a higher price to NAV valuation than it has historically traded at over the last decade. CCD is more frequently traded in the discount territory, and as uncertainty of tariffs and interest rates remain, I believe that CCD can fall back into the discount territory.

Since my last coverage, CCD has released an updated semi-annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.34 per share, while CCD also experienced net realized losses of $0.16 per share. However, there may be a chance that these numbers are negatively skewed due to the timing of the report. The semi-annual report tracks performance data up to April 30th, but market indices declined throughout April in reaction to President Trump's initially aggressive tariff proposals. Therefore, this report doesn't include the following market rally that took place throughout May to mid-July.

Although, it can be tricky to assess because CCD's share price hasn't really participated in the market growth over recent months. In fact, CCD failed to regain any positive momentum back to its previous range at the start of the year, which was around $24 per share. As a result, the report indicates that CCD's NAV has fallen below its year-end 2024 level.

  • 2020: $25.00 per share
  • 2021: $31.73 per share
  • 2022: $20.19 per share
  • 2023: $17.24 per share
  • 2024: $19.24
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $17.56 per share

Looking back over recent years, CCD has struggled to generate positive net investment income levels. This could be related to the fund's underlying use of leverage as part of its operating strategy. The use of leverage currently amounts to 34.32% of assets, which is equivalent to $277M. I consider this to be an aggressive rate of leverage during this period of higher interest rates. Higher interest rates translates to a higher cost of debt, which can negatively impact the fund's spread between the cost of debt and the yield collected from securities. Investors must also consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated towards an investment that doesn't perform as anticipated.

Looking at the chart below, we can see that CCD also continues to remain vulnerable to shifts in the interest rate environment. When interest rates were cut to near zero levels in response to the pandemic in 2020, CCD's share price rapidly increased over 2020 and 2021 due to the low cost of leverage, which helped boost operating margins and made it easier for businesses to grow their operations. Conversely, higher interest rates caused CCD's share price to retract from those highs and it has remained suppressed since.

Additionally, approximately 75.3% of CCD's securities do not have credit ratings. There's a possibility that a greater percentage of these securities are rated as below investment grade, which means that CCD can be exposed to default risks. For reference, ratings below a BBB are considered to be below investment grade. The breakdown of the fund by rating is as follows:

  • A: 2.5%
  • BBB: 11.5%
  • BB: 6.3%
  • B: 3.8%
  • CCC and Below: 0.7%
  • Unrated: 75.3%

S&P Global estimates that leveraged loan defaults can rise to 1.75% in its base scenario, assuming slower economic growth and rising prices that challenge businesses. In their pessimistic scenario, S&P Global sees defaults rising as high as 3.5% by March of 2026, which assumes a continued slowdown in economic growth and disruptions in trade related to tariffs.

Dividend Sustainability

According to the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.195 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.4%. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual distribution of $2.34 per share. The only issue is that CCD's income generation has been inconsistent over the last few years. When market indexes are increasing, CCD seems to have no issue capitalizing on this momentum and realizing gains.

The issue arises when markets are moving sideways or declining. For instance, CCD suffered from a large loss of investment operations totaling $9.24 per share in 2022, which is when market indexes declined double-digits. Going forward, I would love to see CCD's net investment income levels become more consistent. For a fund that puts emphasis on income generation, I see little justification why the fund should be seeing consecutive years of negative net investment income. An improvement here would take away from its reliance on net realized gains to fuel growth and support payouts.

Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that CCD has managed to keep payouts consistent over time. However, I believe this is hurting the fund's NAV over time. Therefore, I believe that CCD should cut its distribution while performance suffers, which would help preserve the NAV and reduce the level of capital erosion shareholders are experiencing. I would much rather see management proactively cut the distribution as a way to help the fund recover, instead of seeing management reactively cut the distribution once conditions become worse.

Additionally, investors should be aware that most of the distributions paid by CCD are funded by a combination of net investment income and net realized gains. Therefore, most of the distributions are classified as ordinary dividend income, which has the highest tax burden for investors. So based on your specific tax bracket, amount of capital invested, and desired income level, a position in CCD would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset any tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on CCD due to the underlying performance weakness in the latest semi-annual reporting. Additionally, the weak earnings has translated to poor distribution coverage. So CCD is currently paying out more than it has actually earned in income, which exacerbates the issue of a declining NAV. I think the fund would benefit from a proactive distribution cut, which may help preserve the NAV. As long as interest rates remain elevated, I believe that CCD will have trouble experiencing positive growth due to the large amount of leverage that is used.

data.title: Valvoline Q3: Growth Story Remains On Track, Breeze Acquisition Stalls
data.description:
Valvoline Inc. (NYSE:VVV) reported the company’s fiscal Q3 results from the April-June period on the 6th of August before market open. The car service center operator and franchisor again showed stable operating momentum in currently standing stores, this time despite higher macroeconomic pressure. At the same time, Valvoline has continued to invest heavily in new locations to fuel attractive long-term earnings growth.

I initiated Valvoline at a Buy rating in my initial May article, titled “Valvoline Has Undervalued Franchise Expansion Potential”. The stock has since returned 10%, very slightly above the S&P 500’s 7% gain.

Valvoline Q3: Good Operating Momentum Continues

Valvoline showed a good continuation of recent operational momentum in Q3. The company reported revenues of $439.0 million, up by 4.2% on the surface and showing much better underlying 11.7% growth when adjusted for the company’s previous refranchising transaction. The topline result beat Wall Street’s consensus by a very slight $2.4 million margin.

The topline growth reflects two key factors – stable, quite high same-store sales growth and Valvoline’s continued franchise expansion. The first factor is a major positive – the consumer sentiment was low throughout the April-June period and Valvoline notes driven miles to have been down in the U.S. during the quarter, but Valvoline regardless continued to experience healthy traffic in a more challenging environment. Same-store sales growth came in at a slightly slower but still good 4.9% level, underlining Valvoline’s defensive nature. The same-store sales momentum continues Valvoline’s long track record of above-inflation growth in the metric, being very valuable to long-term growth expectations and the earnings outlook.

Franchise expansion is also a key positive – Valvoline added 46 system-wide locations during the quarter, including 33 company-operated stores and 13 franchised stores when considering Valvoline’s six location acquisition from a franchisee. The additions push Valvoline’s system-wide stores to a total of 2124 after the quarter as the company still eyes a total of 160-185 openings during FY2025. The franchise’s growth story remains on track, and the high share of company-operated store additions should increase total earnings especially well.

Valvoline’s margin performance was stable in Q3 – adjusted EBITDA came in at $129.5 million at a 29.5% margin, showing a 30 basis point gain year-on-year. Valvoline’s gross margin had slight expansion year-on-year through improved labor management, but the performance was mostly offset by ongoing technology investments in SG&A. The adjusted EPS of $0.47 beat Wall Street’s consensus by two cents, largely coming in as could be expected.

With just one quarter left in Valvoline’s fiscal year, the company narrowed the year’s outlook. Overall, Valvoline’s FY2025 guidance remains very similar, showing very small mid-point boosts in same-store sales growth, net revenues, adjusted EBITDA, and share buybacks.

The quarter continued Valvoline’s growth story in an expectable but encouraging manner – macroeconomic turbulence from an uncertain consumer and higher tariffs hasn’t caused noteworthy pressure on traffic or margins. Valvoline’s capital allocation remains focused on significant growth-related capital expenditures as the company continues to open new stores, as well as on a modest amount of share buybacks.

The Breeze Autocare Acquisition Is Still Pending

Valvoline’s previously announced acquisition of around 200 Oil Changers -branded stores from Breeze Autocare is still pending. The company previously expected to close the transaction by the end of September in FY2025, but the Q3 earnings call suggested some stalling in the deal – Valvoline now hopes to close the transaction in late Q4 or in early fiscal 2026.

The FTC’s regulatory review is still causing some uncertainty around the transaction. The acquisition could concentrate too much market power on Valvoline in some markets, causing ongoing antitrust concerns. Valvoline is working with FTC for the transaction to go through, but Valvoline now flashed the potential need to divest certain stores in order to gain FTC approval.

The transaction’s stalling is in my opinion a negative, albeit a relatively small one; the acquisition’s 10.7 EV/EBITDA is fairly accretive when compared to Valvoline’s own 13.6 EV/EBITDA, but the discrepancy in valuation isn’t massive. The deal still seems likely to go through despite FTC’s increased scrutiny. Most negatively, if Valvoline does need to divest stores, the acquisition would effectively become slightly less attractive as store divestments would likely come at quite a disappointing price.

Updated Valuation

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model slightly for Valvoline. I still assume the Breeze Autocare acquisition to go through without noteworthy divestments in the DCF model, and have kept revenue my estimates similar overall. I again assume Valvoline to reach 3800 total stores by FY2034, but now assume a very slightly higher FY2024-FY2034 same-store sales CAGR of 2.7%.

I still expect Valvoline’s adjusted EBITDA margin to ultimately stabilize at 26% and have kept my cash flow conversion estimates the same. My previous article outlined DCF model assumptions in more detail.

The estimates put Valvoline’s fair value at $56.2, 48% above the stock price at the time of writing; the stock remains undervalued, as Valvoline’s growth story remains on track.

The fair value estimate is down slightly from $56.6 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.34%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6.89% interest rate, annualized from Valvoline’s Q3 interest expenses and debt. I still assume a fair 25% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.22% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.74. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity stands at 7.89% and the WACC at 7.34%.

Takeaway

Valvoline reported solid Q3 results in a more uncertain macroeconomic backdrop. The company continues to expand, meanwhile the Breeze Autocare acquisition is still pending due to FTC’s investigation. Valvoline may have to divest some locations for the transaction to go through. With the growth story still on track, Valvoline remains undervalued. I remain with a Buy rating for the stock.
